does: This paper demonstrates that recasting harmful requests as adversarial poetry creates a universal jailbreak mechanism bypassing safety measures across 25 frontier LLMs. The authors convert 1,200 harmful prompts from the MLCommons dataset into poetic form using a standardized meta-prompt, achieving average attack success rates of 62% for hand-crafted poems and 43% for automated conversions, compared to non-poetic baselines. The attacks transfer effectively across multiple risk domains including CBRN, manipulation, cyber-offence, and loss-of-control scenarios defined by MLCommons and EU Code of Practice taxonomies. Safety outputs are evaluated using an ensemble of three open-weight LLM judges validated against human-labeled stratified subsets, with success rates for some providers exceeding 90%, demonstrating a systematic vulnerability in both proprietary and open-weight models regardless of safety training approach.
connects: This work extends the emerging field of adversarial prompt injection and jailbreaking research that has documented LLMs' susceptibility to reformulation attacks. It builds on prior work showing that certain prompt structures and stylistic choices can circumvent safety training, while contributing novel evidence that poetic formatting—a seemingly benign stylistic transformation—functions as a universal bypass. The paper connects to broader research on adversarial examples in neural networks and safety evaluation frameworks like MLCommons taxonomies and EU regulations, synthesizing these with the specific challenge of LLM robustness. It responds to the gap in understanding why certain linguistic framings prove effective against multiple safety mechanisms across disparate model architectures and training regimes.
enables: These findings point toward fundamental questions about why LLMs treat poetic framing as semantically distinct from harmful intent, suggesting future work on safety training methods that generalize across stylistic variations. The systematic nature of the vulnerability enables development of more comprehensive red-teaming and evaluation protocols that test adversarial robustness beyond standard benchmarks. The work establishes poetic reformulation as a baseline jailbreak for comparative safety research and motivates investigation into whether safety mechanisms can be hardened against form-invariant attacks. These results support calls for safety evaluation that accounts for paraphrasing and stylistic variation, potentially leading to novel training approaches that maintain refuse-to-comply behavior across all reasonable reformulations of harmful requests.
